text:
Have you ever stared at a new codebase 
feeling completely lost? Hundreds of files,

thousands of functions, and no clear starting 
point. What if you had an AI that could read the

entire codebase and transform it into a simple 
beginner-friendly tutorial explaining exactly

how everything works? I built an AI-powered 
system that does exactly that, and it took

me just one afternoon. Let me show you.
Here is the GitHub repository for the

Model Context Protocol Python SDK. Model 
Context Protocol is super hot right now.

It is the new standard for the AI agent 
communication everyone is buzzing about,

but how does it actually work? The repo has dozens 
of Python files, minimal documentation, and feels

intimidating at first glance. All I need to do 
is enter the URL of this repository, configure

a few simple options like the project name, 
output directory, and specify files to exclude.

After hitting start, I will just wait for a few 
minutes and boom! A complete beginner-friendly

tutorial is ready. Suddenly everyone can 
easily understand this complex codebase.

First, there is a clear, concise overview. 
It explains that the SDK helps developers

build apps that communicate via the Model Context 
Protocol, handling everything from message formats

to connection management. Next, it identifies the 
key concepts. There are nine core abstractions,

each clearly defined and expanded. And my 
favorite part? This visual map clearly showing

how all the components connect. The command 
line interface runs the server, the server

manages resources and tools, and the sessions 
utilize transports. Everything suddenly clicks!

Want to build this power for yourself? Really 
become the hero who makes complex code accessible

to everyone? This isn't just another simple prompt 
to ChatGPT. Basic prompts don't cut it for the

entire codebase. What we are talking about here 
is genuine system design powered by a tiny but

mighty framework called PocketFlow. With just 100 
lines of code, PocketFlow simplifies complex AI

workflows dramatically. And the real magic isn't 
just simplicity, it's enabling a revolutionary

new development paradigm called agentic coding. 
You design the workflow and AI does all the heavy

lifting of writing the code for you. Imagine 
that you can let AI agents code AI agents! This

is the fastest and most productive development 
paradigm of 2025. I designed this workflow and

AI has implemented everything for me, and this 
is how I built this entire system in just one

afternoon. And the best part here is that this 
workflow works for any repository, not just MCP

Python SDK. AutoGen, LangChain, PyTorch, OpenAI, 
Manual... you name it. Any codebase becomes very

easy to understand. By the end of this video, you 
will know exactly how to build your own AI-powered

codebase knowledge builder using PocketFlow and 
agentic coding. You will design the system and the

AI will write a codebase for you. And yes, you can 
do all of that in just one afternoon exactly like

I did. Are you ready to never fear exploring 
a new codebase again? Let's dive right in.

We've seen how powerful this AI-driven codebase 
knowledge builder can be, turning intimidating

codebases into clear, intuitive explanations 
in just minutes. But let's step back for a

moment and talk about why we so desperately 
need such a solution. If you're a developer,

you've been there. You join a new team, excited 
and eager, only to be handed a GitHub repository

with hundreds of files. Your heart sinks. Or 
perhaps you find an exciting open source project

you want to contribute to, but where do you even 
get started? You open files at random, search

through functions, desperately trying to trace 
execution paths. Hours pass, days pass, and yet we

are still stuck feeling overwhelmed and confused. 
This isn't just frustrating, it is a massive waste

of time and talent. Did you know the ratio of 
time developers spend reading versus writing

code is well over 10 to 1? Think about this: more 
than half the day, every day, spent figuring out

basic questions like "What does this class even 
do?", "How are these components connected?",

"Why did this original developer design it 
this way?", "What is the big picture here?"

But wait, you might ask, why bother building a 
new system if we already have tools like Cursor or

GitHub Copilot? Let's put Cursor to the test here. 
We open up the Python MCP SDK repository and let's

ask it a simple question like "Can you explain 
what happens under the hood after I define an

addition tool in MCP server?" It gives us bullet 
points about how a Fast MCP server initializes,

how to register, how JSON schemas are generated. 
Lots of these technical terms like tool managers,

resource manager, transport. Sure, it covers the 
basics but also throws around new concepts we've

never seen before. There's no foundational 
explanation of what the schema even means

and how all these things tie together. It is 
basically repeating random code-level details,

not teaching us the bigger picture. We don't 
just want a list of what code does, we need

to know why it's set up this way and how each 
piece connects. This is the critical difference.

A single AI prompt often gives us disjointed, 
surface-level details. It's like rummaging through

a puzzle box for random pieces. Compare that 
to the structured tutorial approach. Instead of

dumping function names and cryptic references, we 
start with a top-level view: what the system does,

how the core abstractions work, and then 
methodically zoom in on each part step by step.

Everything fits together in a coherent mental 
model. We keep the user engaged by making it easy

to follow, providing real context, and linking 
each concept to how it is used in practice. That's

why a system like this matters. If you ever spent 
hours just trying to decipher someone else's code,

you know how frustrating random bits of info 
can be. We don't need more code snippets,

we need a guide. We need a big picture first 
approach. This distinction matters because

understanding code is fundamentally about reverse 
engineering someone else's mental model. Currently

AI struggles because it lacks this systematic 
approach. Understanding someone else's code

doesn't have to be painful. With the right AI 
system, it becomes effortless. Now let's see

exactly how you can build this yourself using 
PocketFlow and agentic coding, and transform

the way you interact with new codebases forever.
We've seen how current AI tools aren't sufficient.

They struggle with seeing the big picture and 
connecting all these pieces clearly. So how do we

build a complete, sophisticated AI system capable 
of truly understanding a codebase quickly? Here is

the good news: I built this entire codebase 
knowledge builder in just one afternoon,

and I'm about to show you exactly how. My secret 
weapon is PocketFlow. It's a tiny yet mighty AI

workflow framework in just 100 lines of code. 
Anyone can understand PocketFlow in just minutes.

But don't let its small size fool you. PocketFlow 
uses a simple yet expressive graph abstraction,

similar in concept to popular frameworks like 
LangChain or N8N, but dramatically simpler and

more transparent. With PocketFlow, you can 
effortlessly create multi-agent systems,

sophisticated workflows, map-reduce operations, 
tasks you might normally find associated with

heavy, complex AI frameworks. PocketFlow is 
incredibly minimal, offering complete clarity

without sacrificing power. Check out PocketFlow's 
documentation and cookbook to learn more.

PocketFlow's elegant, minimal design makes 
it the perfect foundation for agentic coding,

a new development paradigm that's transforming 
how we build large language model systems. With

agentic coding, you focus exclusively on 
high-level system design. You design the

workflows and define clear process steps where 
AI handles all the implementation details and

writes the actual code. Think of yourself 
as an architect creating blueprints. Your

responsibility is creating strategic designs, 
determining the structure, connections, and

overall vision. You then hand these blueprints to 
an AI construction team that builds exactly what

you've envisioned. Because PocketFlow's structure 
is so clear and straightforward, AI agents quickly

comprehend your design and efficiently 
handle all the technical implementation.

I've developed comprehensive PocketFlow 
documentation that explains how to use it

effectively, along with best practices 
for agentic coding. Additionally,

I created detailed tutorials demonstrating how to 
set up an agentic coding environment using popular

tools like Cursor. The key to making agentic 
coding work seamlessly with these tools is to

provide a rule file with PocketFlow documentation. 
I've prepared such a project template that you can

use immediately. Simply clone this project in 
your Cursor window and you are ready to start.

You can verify Cursor understands PocketFlow by 
asking "What is PocketFlow?" in the chat mode,

and you will see how effortlessly Cursor 
AI agent comprehends the framework.

Now let me briefly explain how PocketFlow 
works using a simple intuitive analogy.

Imagine a well-organized kitchen. Nodes 
in PocketFlow are like cooking stations,

each one handles a specific task such as 
chopping, cooking, or plating. Flow acts

as the recipe, telling each station which step 
to perform next. It looks at the conditions:

if vegetables are chopped, proceed to the cooking 
station; if the meal is cooked, move on to the

plating. Meanwhile, the shared store functions 
as a central countertop holding ingredients for

everyone to see and use. When a node runs, it 
calls its prep method to collect exactly what

it needs from the shared store. It calls its 
exec method to handle the specialized task,

maybe chopping or grilling. Finally, it calls 
post to place the completed work back onto the

shared store, making it available for the next 
station. Now this simple structure—nodes, flow,

and shared store—keeps PocketFlow incredibly 
transparent. You can glance at the code and see

exactly how your AI workflow is laid out without 
any hidden complexity. Everything happens right

where you can see it, making PocketFlow powerful 
yet easy to understand. Now let's dive deeper

and see exactly how we can use PocketFlow to 
build your own AI codebase knowledge builder.

We've seen how current AI tools can't fully grasp 
an entire codebase. They miss the big picture and

crucial relationships. So how do we create an 
AI system that truly understands code using

PocketFlow? The key lies in thoughtful system 
design, specifically we design a workflow that

follows what I call the "overview-zoomin" 
approach. This method involves two clear,

powerful stages. In the overview stage, our 
AI systematically scans the entire codebase,

identifies core abstractions, and uncovers 
how these abstractions relate to each

other. It then logically organizes them into a 
structured, intuitive tutorial outline. Imagine

this as taking a wide-angle snapshot, quickly 
capturing the project's overall architecture,

critical components, and their interactions. Then 
comes the zooming stage. Here, the AI drills down

into each individual abstraction, providing 
comprehensive and detailed explanations,

illustrative examples, and practical code 
snippets. This method ensures learners fully

grasp each component before moving on. This 
structured approach closely mirrors the way

experienced developers naturally explore and learn 
complex code, providing both clarity and depth.

Now let's ask Cursor to help us make our initial 
pass of such design. Cursor offers two modes: chat

mode and agent mode. At the design stage, chat 
mode is particularly effective. In Cursor's chat

mode, let's type "Help me write a detailed design 
doc for an AI-powered codebase knowledge builder.

First, it provides an overview by identifying core 
abstractions, determining their relationships, and

sequencing them logically for tutorial clarity. 
Then it zooms into each abstraction individually,

crafting detailed explanations and practical 
examples." This stage isn't necessarily agentic

coding yet. It's about clearly designing the 
system and setting explicit guidelines. The better

your design at this phase, the simpler and more 
effective the subsequent agentic coding becomes.

Then boom! In just a few minutes, 
Cursor AI generates a comprehensive,

clear design document outlining exactly how the AI 
codebase knowledge builder will function. Let me

walk you through the flow design using the MCP 
Python SDK as our example and showing how the

overview-zooming strategy unfolds. We start with 
"fetch repo." This node's job is straightforward:

it downloads all of the relevant Python files 
from the MCP GitHub repository. Once we have

all those files, we move on to the "identify 
abstractions" node. Here, the AI scans through

the codebase, spotting key concepts such as 
MCP protocol types, client server sessions,

communication transports, the Fast MCP 
server, resources, tools, prompts, context,

and the command-line interface. This is our 
wide-angle overview. We are mapping out the major

building blocks that define how the SDK works.
With those abstractions identified, the process

continues to the "analyze relationships." In 
this stage, AI figures out how these pieces

depend on or interact with each other, like how 
the Fast MCP server manages resources, tools,

and prompts, and how client-server sessions 
rely on protocol types. By clarifying these

connections, we build a crucial framework that 
shows exactly how everything fits together.

Then comes the "order chapters" node, where 
we transform the newly uncovered relationships

into a logical learning path. The AI decides the 
best teaching sequence, perhaps starting with the

command-line interface since it's the entry point, 
then moving on to the server layer, sessions,

transports, and so on. It's basically turning 
a jumble of ideas into a neat chapter outline.

Finally, we get into the "write chapters" node, 
which is a batch node that does exactly what its

name suggests: it zooms in on each abstraction 
one by one. This is where the AI writes thorough

explanations, offers concrete code examples, 
and breaks down real usage patterns for each

abstraction. This is the stage of the detail 
lens, giving learners everything they need to

truly understand each piece of the SDK. Now 
that we've established the entire design,

the next section is where we actually have the 
AI implement it, this time harnessing Cursor's

powerful agent mode to generate a workflow 
and code all these nodes automatically.

All right, we've clearly outlined our system 
design using the overview-zooming approach. Now

it's time to bring it to life. Imagine this: 
instead of merely coding every single piece,

spending days or even weeks to implement, we 
are about to do something very differently.

This is exactly where agentic coding shines. 
Let's step into the magic of agentic coding.

We switch Cursor to agent mode and type one 
simple but powerful instruction: "Implement

based on the design doc." Start agentic coding 
and hit send. Boom! In just about 5 minutes,

Cursor has generated all the code and implemented 
all these nodes for our PocketFlow workflow.

First, the "fetch repository" node acts like 
a librarian. Its preprocessing steps involve

grabbing the GitHub URL from the shared store. 
During execution, it downloads all relevant files,

skipping unnecessary folders like tests or 
documentation. In the postprocessing step,

the downloaded codebase is saved back into shared 
store for the next node. "Identify abstractions"

serves as our analyst. In its preprocessing 
step, it takes the downloaded codebase. The

execution phase uses AI to identify major classes, 
modules, and patterns—our key abstractions. The

postprocessing step includes saving these 
identified abstractions along with short

descriptions back into the shared store. Then, 
"analyze relationships" connects the dots. Its

preprocessing step involves loading abstractions 
and the original codebase. During execution,

it analyzes relationships such as 
imports, method calls, inheritance,

and composition. The postprocessing step 
produces a clear map of these relationships,

storing them back into shared store.
"Order chapters" acts as our organizer. In its

preprocessing step, it collects the abstractions 
and their relationships. During execution,

it determines the most logical sequence for 
presenting the concepts. Its postprocessing

step posts this recommended chapter order back 
into shared store. "Write chapters" functions

as our storyteller. Its preprocessing step 
involves preparing batches for each abstraction.

For execution, I did some prompt engineering to 
refine the instructions based on my own personal

experience creating tutorials for system design. 
Each chapter starts with a motivating use case and

clearly explains key abstractions using concise 
code snippets under 20 lines. Practical examples,

diagrams, and analogies are also included 
to enhance understanding and maintain smooth

transitions. Finally, "combine tutorial" 
is our publisher. Its preprocessing step

involves gathering all chapters, diagrams, and 
outlines from our shared store. During execution,

it compiles everything into a neatly formatted 
folder directory with an index page and visual

overviews. Its postprocessing step stores 
the location of the completed tutorial.

By unleashing agentic coding with Cursor's agent 
mode, it feels like watching ideas spring to life.

Instead of spending days or even weeks coding 
everything manually, we can have a working AI

system in just a few minutes. Now let's run 
the completed system on real-world examples,

effortlessly turning the browser use codebase 
into an engaging, beginner-friendly tutorial.

All right, browser automation is a huge deal right 
now. Everyone is excited about letting AI directly

control browsers, filling forms, scraping data, or 
clicking buttons automatically. But let's face it,

these codebases can get pretty complex. So 
let's use our newly built AI-powered codebase

knowledge builder to peek inside one of the 
hottest browser automation frameworks. Boom!

In just a few minutes, here is the exact 
tutorial our AI assistant just generated,

and honestly, the results are pretty amazing.
First off, we get a clear high-level explanation.

Browser use lets you act like a skilled assistant 
who can handle tasks on the web for you. No more

repetitive manual browsing. Behind the scenes, it 
leverages a large model as its brain to understand

your instructions and decide exactly how to 
interact with the web pages. To achieve this,

it cleverly manages browser sessions, understands 
web page structures, and facilitates smooth

communication between instructions and AI's 
actions. And the best part? Our AI didn't

stop at explaining things in words. Check out 
this visual diagram it automatically created to

show how all components fit together. You 
immediately see how everything connects.

Let's wrap this up. Here are the three main 
takeaways from today's video. First, understanding

large codebases demands a holistic approach. Many 
existing AI tools provide partial or file-by-file

insights but lack the big picture needed to grasp 
the entire repository. For truly deep knowledge,

you need a system that can systematically fetch 
all relevant files, identify core abstractions,

and uncover the relationships between them.
Second, system design matters for large language

models. Even the most advanced large language 
models can get stuck delivering fragmented or

out of context information when faced with large 
repositories. Our overview to zooming strategy

ensures that we maintain clarity, context, 
and intuitive understanding at each step,

from a high-level architectural snapshot down 
to the individual modules. By orchestrating

tasks in a methodical sequence like fetching, 
parsing, and analyzing code, we keep the large

language model focused and provide it with 
the right information at the right time.

Finally, PocketFlow and agentic coding take this 
structured approach to the next level. PocketFlow

lets you design complex pipelines with total 
transparency and minimal overhead. By breaking

tasks into manageable nodes and flows, it 
clarifies what data the large model sees

at each step, preventing information overload 
and ensuring results stay consistent. Agentic

coding complements this by letting you focus on 
the big picture system design while AI handles

the nitty-gritty implementation details. You 
outline the flow—fetch the repository, identify

abstractions, analyze relationships, create 
tutorials—and the AI fills in the rest. This means

you can design and deploy multi-step AI workflows 
in hours, not days. Never again feel overwhelmed

by complex codebases. Now you have the insights, 
strategies, and tools to transform any repository

into a structured, easy-to-follow tutorial. If 
this video was helpful, please like and subscribe.
